text: When designing a REST API, clear best practices ensure reliability and ease of use. Endpoints should be named logically, such as /users or /orders, using nouns rather than verbs to represent resources. Use standard HTTP status codes: 200 for success, 201 for created resources, 400 for bad requests, and 404 for not found. Always include proper error messages in responses to help developers debug. Document your API thoroughly, explaining each endpoint, its parameters, and expected responses. Version your API from the start, for example, /v1/users, so changes don't break existing users. A well-designed API saves time and frustration for both you and your developers.
